question: Which statement best distinguishes sensation from perception?

options.A: Sensation organizes meaning, whereas perception detects raw stimulation.
options.B: Sensation encodes incoming stimulation, whereas perception organizes and interprets it.
options.C: Sensation occurs only in vision, whereas perception occurs in every sense.
options.D: Sensation is always conscious, whereas perception is always unconscious.

related_info: The lecture defines sensory processes as encoding stimulation through the sensory organs, while perception is the brain's active organization and interpretation of that input.
why_high_yield: High yield because sensation-versus-perception is the first conceptual distinction in the perception lecture and anchors many later examples.
source_refs: Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (all Module 1 subjects included; Lachs required reading listed)
Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, pp. 3-6
Lachs, L. (2023). Multi-modal perception (required reading)

question: The conversion of environmental stimulation into electrical signals that the nervous system can process is called ______.
related_info: Transduction refers to converting sensory input into neural signals, which is one of the basic functions of sensory systems.
why_high_yield: High yield because transduction is a core definition-level concept from the opening part of the perception lecture.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, p. 3

question: When a stimulus is too weak to be detected at all, it falls below the ______.
related_info: The lecture defines the absolute threshold as the point below which sensory information is too weak to be detected.
why_high_yield: High yield because threshold concepts are classic perception test items and appear early in the lecture's explanation of why not all sensory input becomes conscious perception.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, p. 6
Lachs, L. (2023). Multi-modal perception (required reading)

question: After sitting in a room with a strong perfume for several minutes, you barely notice the smell. This is an example of ______.
related_info: Sensory adaptation is diminished sensitivity to a stimulus after prolonged exposure.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture presents sensory adaptation as one of the fundamental reasons perception is selective rather than a perfect copy of the environment.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, p. 6

question: A student is so focused on texting while walking that she fails to notice a friend waving directly in front of her. This best illustrates ______.
related_info: Inattentional blindness occurs when attention determines what is perceived and what is missed, even when the stimulus is present.
why_high_yield: High yield because it turns the lecture's abstract claim that not all stimulation becomes perception into a vivid applied example.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, p. 6

question: Seeing a broken circle as a complete circle because the mind fills in missing parts is an example of the Gestalt principle of ______.
related_info: Closure is the Gestalt tendency to supply missing elements so that incomplete stimuli are perceived as complete wholes.
why_high_yield: High yield because Gestalt laws are one of the most testable perception subtopics in the slide deck.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, pp. 9-12

question: Reading a messy sentence successfully because you use prior knowledge about language and context is best explained by ______ processing.
related_info: Top-down processing uses existing knowledge and expectations to identify a stimulus efficiently and predictively.
why_high_yield: High yield because top-down versus bottom-up processing is a classic comparison and often appears in example-based questions.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, pp. 13-15
Lachs, L. (2023). Multi-modal perception (required reading)

question: With respect to vision, what remains stable in perceptual constancy?

options.C: our perception of the object's properties

related_info: Perceptual constancy refers to the stability of perception despite changes in the retinal image; for example, perceived size or shape can remain constant.
why_high_yield: High yield because perceptual constancy appears directly in the Exam 1 sample multiple-choice questions.
source_refs: Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (sample question on perceptual constancy)
Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, pp. 16-17
Lachs, L. (2023). Multi-modal perception (required reading)

question: When the two eyes receive slightly different images and the brain uses that difference to judge distance, the relevant cue is ______.
related_info: Retinal disparity is a binocular cue: the greater the difference between the two retinal images, the closer the object tends to be perceived.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture clearly separates binocular and monocular depth cues, a distinction that is easy to test through scenarios.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, pp. 18-21

question: The McGurk effect is strongest evidence that perception is ______.
related_info: The McGurk effect shows that visual information about speech can alter what people hear, demonstrating multisensory perception.
why_high_yield: High yield because multisensory perception is the required reading topic and the McGurk effect is its signature example in the lecture.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, pp. 28-32
Lachs, L. (2023). Multi-modal perception (required reading)

question: Which statement about REM sleep is correct?

options.A: It is the stage in which heart rate and breathing are at their lowest and stable levels.
options.B: It features brain waves similar to wakefulness, rapid eye movements, and muscle paralysis.
options.C: It contains no dreaming at all.
options.D: It is identical to deep stage 3 and 4 sleep.

related_info: REM sleep is marked by wake-like brain activity, rapid eye movements, increases in several physiological measures, and temporary muscle paralysis.
why_high_yield: High yield because REM is one of the clearest definitional contrasts in the sleep lecture and easy to turn into multiple-choice questions.
source_refs: Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (all Module 1 subjects included)
Module 1/Lecture Slides - Can people die from not sleeping.pdf, pp. 3-5

question: Why is sleep especially relevant to learning in this course?

options.A: Because sleep eliminates the need for retrieval practice.
options.B: Because sleep supports memory formation and consolidation.
options.C: Because only REM sleep stores procedural memories.
options.D: Because staying awake longer always improves attention.

related_info: The lecture emphasizes that sleep supports cognitive functions such as memory formation and consolidation, and that sleep after learning can improve later performance.
why_high_yield: High yield because sleep's role in memory bridges Module 1 and Module 2, making it especially likely to appear in integrative exam questions.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Can people die from not sleeping.pdf, pp. 6-8

question: External cues such as light and daily activities that help synchronize circadian rhythms are called ______.
related_info: Zeitgebers are external time-givers, such as light and regular activities, that help align internal rhythms with the 24-hour day.
why_high_yield: High yield because zeitgeber is one of the few specialized terms in the sleep lecture and is tied directly to circadian misalignment.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Can people die from not sleeping.pdf, pp. 13-14

question: Which condition is most directly characterized by repeated breathing interruptions during sleep, loud snoring, and abrupt awakenings?
related_info: The lecture distinguishes sleep apnea from insomnia by emphasizing breathing difficulties, snoring, and abrupt awakenings.
why_high_yield: High yield because this is a straightforward but highly testable distinction within the sleep-problems section.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Can people die from not sleeping.pdf, pp. 18-20

question: Which statement best describes an implicit process?

options.A: It is a mental activity of which we are always consciously aware.
options.B: It is a mental activity that influences us even without conscious awareness.
options.C: It occurs only during dream sleep.
options.D: It is limited to voluntary movement.

related_info: The lecture contrasts explicit processes, which are conscious, with implicit processes, which can influence perception, memory, and action without awareness.
why_high_yield: High yield because explicit-versus-implicit processing is the organizing contrast for the consciousness lecture.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 7-9
Biswas-Diener, R. & Teeny, J. (2023). States of consciousness (required reading)

question: Patient TN could avoid obstacles in a hallway despite being clinically blind. This phenomenon is called ______.
related_info: Blindsight refers to responding to visual information without conscious visual awareness, as in the case described in the lecture.
why_high_yield: High yield because blindsight is the lecture's clearest empirical example showing that perception and awareness can come apart.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 10-12
Biswas-Diener, R. & Teeny, J. (2023). States of consciousness (required reading)

question: The Implicit Association Test is designed to measure ______.
related_info: The lecture describes the IAT as a reaction-time measure of implicit attitudes, such as stereotypes toward social groups.
why_high_yield: High yield because it is a named assessment tool attached to the broader theme of implicit processing.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 17-18

question: When a briefly presented word influences how quickly you identify a later target without your awareness, the effect is called ______.
related_info: Priming occurs when exposure to one stimulus influences a later perception, memory, or response, even without conscious awareness of the prime.
why_high_yield: High yield because priming is a classic introductory psychology effect and an easy way to test implicit processing.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 19-22
Biswas-Diener, R. & Teeny, J. (2023). States of consciousness (required reading)

question: According to the lecture, subliminal advertising appears to influence product choice mainly when ______.

options.A: people are motivated in a relevant way, such as already being thirsty
options.B: people are never motivated at all
options.C: the message is consciously read for a long time
options.D: the product is unrelated to the person's needs

related_info: The lecture notes that subliminal priming can affect purchase intentions only under relevant active states, such as thirst, and cannot simply override people's wishes.
why_high_yield: High yield because it tests a common misconception about unconscious influence and turns it into a nuanced application question.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 21-22

question: Terror management theory proposes that many behaviors are shaped by the tension between ______.

options.A: the desire for variety and the fear of boredom
options.B: the desire for survival and awareness of death
options.C: the id and the superego only

related_info: The lecture explains terror management theory as an unconscious motivation arising from the juxtaposition of the desire to survive and the awareness of death.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture provides a clear named theory plus concrete experiments, which is a common exam combination.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 28-30

question: According to basic emotion theory, which claim is most consistent with the lecture?

options.A: All emotions are purely language-based constructions with no physiological patterning.
options.B: Basic emotions are universal and linked to distinctive physiological or neural profiles.
options.C: Emotions are too socially variable to have any common features.
options.D: Only happiness and sadness are real emotions.

related_info: Basic emotion theory argues that some emotions are universally manifested and recognized and have distinctive biological profiles with adaptive functions.
why_high_yield: High yield because the emotions lecture is organized around the contrast between basic emotion theory and constructivist theory.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - why do we feel emotionally connected to characters in animations.pdf, pp. 3-7
Hwang, H. & Matsumoto, D. (2023). Functions of emotions (required reading)

question: In the circumplex model, emotional experiences are organized mainly along the dimensions of ______.
related_info: The lecture presents the circumplex model as a constructivist account that maps emotions along valence and arousal or activation.
why_high_yield: High yield because it is the most concrete representation of constructivist emotion theory in the slides.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - why do we feel emotionally connected to characters in animations.pdf, pp. 8-10
Hwang, H. & Matsumoto, D. (2023). Functions of emotions (required reading)

question: Which example best illustrates an interpersonal function of emotion?

options.A: Fear redistributes blood flow in preparation for fleeing.
options.B: Gratitude helps build supportive and caring relationships.

related_info: The lecture explains that emotions help people navigate the social world and specifically notes that experienced gratitude can strengthen supportive relationships.
why_high_yield: High yield because the functions-of-emotion framework is required reading material and gratitude is the lecture's clearest relationship example.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - why do we feel emotionally connected to characters in animations.pdf, pp. 15-16
Hwang, H. & Matsumoto, D. (2023). Functions of emotions (required reading)

question: Deliberately visiting a favorite peaceful place in order to feel better before an important meeting is an example of ______.
related_info: Emotion regulation refers to processes by which people influence which emotions they have, when they have them, and how those emotions are experienced or expressed.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture provides a full section of concrete examples that are easy to transform into scenario-based exam questions.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - why do we feel emotionally connected to characters in animations.pdf, pp. 18-21

question: In Pavlov's framework, a bell that originally meant nothing but later triggers salivation after repeated pairings with food becomes the ______.
related_info: A conditioned stimulus is a once-neutral stimulus that acquires the ability to trigger a response after being paired with an unconditioned stimulus.
why_high_yield: High yield because classical-conditioning terminology is one of the most standard and repeatedly tested topic areas in introductory psychology.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - how is it possible to teach pigeons to play ping-pong.pdf, pp. 3-8
Bouton, M. E. (2023). Conditioning and learning (required reading)

question: Feeling sick after eating a food that was paired with illness in the past is the best example of ______.
related_info: Conditioned taste aversion occurs when a taste becomes associated with sickness, leading the organism to avoid or dislike that taste later.
why_high_yield: High yield because it is a memorable real-world application of classical conditioning highlighted in the lecture.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - how is it possible to teach pigeons to play ping-pong.pdf, pp. 11-13
Bouton, M. E. (2023). Conditioning and learning (required reading)

question: Why is extinction central to exposure therapy?

options.A: Because it strengthens a conditioned fear response.
options.B: Because it reduces a conditioned response when the conditioned stimulus is no longer paired with the unconditioned stimulus.
options.C: Because it increases the power of punishment.
options.D: Because it creates new retrieval cues.

related_info: Extinction occurs when a conditioned response weakens because the conditioned stimulus is repeatedly presented without the unconditioned stimulus.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture directly names exposure therapy as an application of extinction, creating an obvious exam link between concept and intervention.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - how is it possible to teach pigeons to play ping-pong.pdf, p. 14
Bouton, M. E. (2023). Conditioning and learning (required reading)

question: In operant conditioning, a consequence that increases the future likelihood of a behavior is called ______.
related_info: Operant conditioning focuses on how consequences change voluntary behavior; reinforcement strengthens behavior, whereas punishment weakens it.
why_high_yield: High yield because reinforcement-versus-punishment is a high-frequency introductory distinction and a core part of the conditioning lecture.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - how is it possible to teach pigeons to play ping-pong.pdf, pp. 15-18
Bouton, M. E. (2023). Conditioning and learning (required reading)

question: Rewarding closer and closer approximations to a desired complex behavior is called ______.
related_info: Shaping teaches a complex behavior gradually by reinforcing successive approximations of the target response.
why_high_yield: High yield because shaping is one of the lecture's clearest applied examples of operant conditioning and links directly to the pigeons theme.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - how is it possible to teach pigeons to play ping-pong.pdf, pp. 19-21

question: Children who imitate an aggressive model less when they saw the model punished demonstrate the importance of ______.

options.B: vicarious reinforcement or punishment in observational learning

related_info: Observational learning does not require direct reinforcement; seeing a model rewarded or punished can change whether the observer imitates the behavior.
why_high_yield: High yield because Bandura-style modeling is a classic introductory psychology finding and the lecture explicitly connects it to Bobo doll evidence.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - how is it possible to teach pigeons to play ping-pong.pdf, pp. 24-29
Bouton, M. E. (2023). Conditioning and learning (required reading)

question: When perception separates an object from its background, the relevant Gestalt idea is ______.
related_info: Figure-ground organization refers to the tendency to separate elements into a focal figure and a background.
why_high_yield: High yield because figure-ground is one of the named Gestalt laws in the perception lecture and is easy to assess visually or verbally.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, pp. 9-11

question: When one object partly blocks another and is therefore seen as closer, the monocular depth cue is ______.
related_info: Interposition is a monocular depth cue in which nearer objects obscure parts of farther objects.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture gives a clear list of depth cues and exams often ask students to map a concrete scene to the right cue.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, pp. 18-22

question: Seeing a hill as steeper when you are tired or carrying a heavy backpack best supports the idea of ______.
related_info: Embodied or action-specific perception means the body and its ability to act shape how the environment is perceived.
why_high_yield: High yield because the heavy-backpack example is one of the lecture's most memorable perception applications.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Why does the moon look so big near the horizonpdf, pp. 33-35
Lachs, L. (2023). Multi-modal perception (required reading)

question: Which sleep stage is described as deep sleep and as the least responsive to outside stimulation?
related_info: The lecture identifies stages 3 and 4 of non-REM sleep as deep sleep, with minimal responsiveness to external stimulation.
why_high_yield: High yield because sleep-stage contrasts are straightforward but central test material from the sleep lecture.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Can people die from not sleeping.pdf, pp. 3-5

question: If people are deprived of REM sleep, what is likely to happen when they can sleep normally again?

options.A: They show REM rebound and spend more time in REM sleep.
options.B: They permanently lose the ability to dream.
options.D: They become unable to consolidate memories at all.

related_info: REM rebound refers to increased REM sleep after REM deprivation.
why_high_yield: High yield because REM rebound is a named sleep concept that is easy to test with a short scenario.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Can people die from not sleeping.pdf, p. 5

question: Working night shifts and then trying to sleep at biologically unusual times most directly illustrates ______.
related_info: Circadian misalignment occurs when sleep-wake timing is out of sync with the body's biological rhythms, as in shift work or social jet lag.
why_high_yield: High yield because the sleep lecture emphasizes modern causes of poor sleep and the health risks linked to circadian disruption.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - Can people die from not sleeping.pdf, pp. 19-22

question: Alcohol is classified in the consciousness lecture as a ______.
related_info: The lecture groups psychoactive drugs into depressants, stimulants, and hallucinogens, with alcohol listed as a depressant.
why_high_yield: High yield because altered states of consciousness are introduced through a simple but exam-friendly drug classification scheme.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 4-6
Biswas-Diener, R. & Teeny, J. (2023). States of consciousness (required reading)

question: In the lecture, meditation is described mainly as ______.

options.B: focusing on a single target to achieve awareness and present-moment attention
options.C: evidence that all cognition is unconscious

related_info: Meditation is presented as sustained focus on a single target, with benefits for stress, hypertension, pain, and sleep quality.
why_high_yield: High yield because meditation is one of the main altered states named in the consciousness lecture and ties to well-being applications.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 5-6

question: Noticing your own name in a noisy room even while focused on another conversation is the classic ______ effect.
related_info: The cocktail party effect shows that some processing of unattended information occurs outside focal awareness.
why_high_yield: High yield because it is one of the lecture's clearest examples of unconscious or implicit processing in everyday life.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 13-15

question: How is skilled cycling, swimming, or driving described once it has been extensively practiced?

options.A: As an explicit process that requires constant conscious control
options.B: As an implicit or automatic procedural memory process
options.C: As a type of sensory adaptation
options.D: As a form of experimental control

related_info: The lecture explains that retrieval of procedural skills becomes implicit and automatic with extensive practice.
why_high_yield: High yield because it connects implicit processing to everyday skilled performance, not just laboratory demonstrations.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - are you truly in control, or is your brain on autopilot.pdf, pp. 15-16

question: Which statement best distinguishes a mood from an emotion?

options.A: A mood is usually briefer and more physiologically intense than an emotion.
options.B: A mood is an emotional state extended over time, whereas an emotion is a briefer episode.
options.C: A mood is always unconscious, whereas an emotion is always conscious.

related_info: The lecture defines emotion as a brief episode involving experience, behavior, and physiology, while mood is an emotional state extended in time.
why_high_yield: High yield because emotion-versus-mood is a foundational distinction at the start of the emotions lecture.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - why do we feel emotionally connected to characters in animations.pdf, pp. 1-2

question: Which of the following is presented as a secondary emotion rather than a primary basic emotion?
related_info: The lecture lists emotions such as guilt, gratitude, and nostalgia as secondary emotions that rely more heavily on social experience and higher cognition.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture explicitly contrasts primary basic emotions with secondary emotions.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - why do we feel emotionally connected to characters in animations.pdf, pp. 10-11

question: The Little Albert experiment is used in the lecture as an example of ______.
related_info: Little Albert is presented as a classic example of fear conditioning, in which a previously neutral stimulus comes to elicit fear after pairing.
why_high_yield: High yield because it is the signature human example attached to fear conditioning in the lecture.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - how is it possible to teach pigeons to play ping-pong.pdf, pp. 8-10
Bouton, M. E. (2023). Conditioning and learning (required reading)

question: Giving tokens that can later be exchanged for privileges in order to increase desired behaviors is called a ______.
related_info: A token economy is an operant-conditioning system in which tokens function as reinforcers that can later be traded for desired outcomes.
why_high_yield: High yield because it is one of the lecture's clearest real-world applications of operant conditioning.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - how is it possible to teach pigeons to play ping-pong.pdf, pp. 21-23

question: Children becoming less afraid of dogs after watching a fearless peer interact with one is used in the lecture as an example of ______.
related_info: The lecture describes therapy examples in which observing a model can reduce fear and encourage approach behavior.
why_high_yield: High yield because it extends observational learning from Bobo doll aggression to a practical therapeutic context.
source_refs: Module 1/Lecture Slides - how is it possible to teach pigeons to play ping-pong.pdf, pp. 27-29
Bouton, M. E. (2023). Conditioning and learning (required reading)
